subject: Space Launch
executive_summary:
Space launch technology is experiencing unprecedented competition between the United States and China, with 2025 marking record-breaking years for both nations. SpaceX shattered its own annual record with 165 orbital Falcon 9 missions plus five suborbital Starship test flights, representing approximately 85% of total U.S. launches and nearly double China's output. China reached 92 orbital launches in 2025, surpassing its previous record of 68 set in 2024 and demonstrating the ability to coordinate four launches from four different spaceports within 96 hours. The U.S. Space Force awarded $13.7 billion in National Security Space Launch Phase 3 contracts to SpaceX ($5.9 billion, 28 missions), ULA ($5.3 billion, 19 missions), and Blue Origin ($2.4 billion, 7 missions), establishing the competitive framework for military launch through the late 2020s.

Reusable launch technology remains the critical differentiator where the U.S. holds a commanding lead. The United States now has two vehicles with demonstrated orbital-class booster recovery -- SpaceX's Falcon family (routinely landing and reflying boosters) and Blue Origin's New Glenn (first successful recovery in November 2025). SpaceX's Starship program achieved its first fully successful suborbital test flights in August and October 2025 after earlier partial failures, setting the stage for potential orbital flights in 2026. China is aggressively pursuing reusability through both state-owned CASC and multiple commercial companies (LandSpace, Space Pioneer, Galactic Energy, CAS Space), but two late-2025 orbital-class recovery attempts failed, indicating China remains 5-7 years behind SpaceX in operational reusable launch.

ULA's Vulcan Centaur rocket, the planned Atlas V/Delta IV replacement for national security launches, has encountered operational challenges that threaten the Pentagon's assured-access-to-space strategy. Only one Vulcan launch occurred in 2025 (versus a planned ten), and the Space Force paused national security missions on Vulcan following a repeated booster anomaly. This has increased reliance on SpaceX for time-sensitive military launches, raising single-provider risk concerns. China's military space capabilities continue to expand through the Yaogan satellite constellation and classified national security payloads, with the country demonstrating surge launch capacity and multi-spaceport coordination.

key_findings.headline: SpaceX sets sixth consecutive annual launch record with 165 orbital missions in 2025
key_findings.detail: SpaceX completed 165 Falcon 9 orbital launches in 2025, shattering its previous record and representing approximately 85% of total U.S. launches. The company averaged one launch nearly every other day, including six National Security Space Launch missions for the Space Force. Additionally, five suborbital Starship test flights were conducted, with the final two (August and October) achieving full success after earlier partial failures.

key_findings.headline: U.S. Space Force awards $13.7 billion in NSSL Phase 3 launch contracts
key_findings.detail: The Space Force awarded nearly $14 billion in National Security Space Launch Phase 3 Lane 2 contracts: SpaceX received $5.9 billion for 28 missions (52%), ULA got $5.3 billion for 19 missions (35%), and Blue Origin received $2.4 billion for 7 missions (13%). SpaceX is scheduled for five NSSL missions through September 2026 for the Space Force and National Reconnaissance Office.

key_findings.headline: ULA Vulcan suffers operational setbacks, Space Force pauses national security missions
key_findings.detail: ULA's Vulcan Centaur rocket completed only one launch in 2025 versus ten planned. Its first national security mission occurred August 12, but the Space Force subsequently paused all Vulcan national security launches due to a repeated booster anomaly. This forced the transfer of at least one mission to SpaceX, highlighting single-provider risk in the U.S. assured access to space architecture.

key_findings.headline: China reaches 92 orbital launches in 2025, demonstrating surge capacity from four spaceports
key_findings.detail: China conducted 92 orbital launch attempts in 2025, far surpassing its previous record of 68 in 2024. In a remarkable demonstration of surge capacity, China executed four orbital missions from four different spaceports (Hainan, Taiyuan, Jiuquan, Xichang) within 96 hours, and set a record of three launches within 19 hours. Military-relevant payloads included Yaogan reconnaissance satellites and classified national security missions.

key_findings.headline: China's reusable rocket attempts fail but multiple commercial players advance rapidly
key_findings.detail: China attempted two orbital-class rocket first-stage recoveries in late 2025 but failed to retrieve the boosters. However, multiple commercial companies are advancing rapidly: LandSpace (Zhuque-3, 21.3-ton payload), Space Pioneer (Tianlong-3), Galactic Energy (Pallas-1), and CAS Space (Kinetica-2) all have reusable rockets in development or ready for debut flights. State-owned CASC is developing 4-meter and 5-meter reusable vehicles.

key_findings.headline: Blue Origin achieves first orbital-class booster recovery with New Glenn
key_findings.detail: Blue Origin successfully launched and recovered the first stage of its New Glenn rocket on November 13, 2025, making it only the second organization (after SpaceX) to achieve orbital-class booster recovery. The company received $2.4 billion in NSSL Phase 3 contracts for seven military launches, diversifying the U.S. launch provider base beyond SpaceX-ULA.

recent_developments.headline: China caps record year with 92 orbital launches including military satellites
recent_developments.summary: China completed its 91st and 92nd orbital launches of 2025, setting a new national record that exceeds the previous record of 68 (set in 2024) by 35%. The launches included classified Yaogan military reconnaissance satellites, technology demonstration missions, and commercial constellation deployments. China demonstrated the ability to launch from four spaceports within 96 hours, indicating robust surge capacity for military contingencies.
recent_developments.impact: China's rapidly increasing launch cadence and demonstrated surge capacity from multiple spaceports enables rapid reconstitution of military satellite constellations and responsive space operations during a crisis.

recent_developments.headline: Blue Origin achieves first New Glenn launch and booster recovery
recent_developments.summary: Blue Origin successfully launched New Glenn for the first time and recovered the first-stage booster on November 13, 2025, establishing the United States as the only nation with two proven orbital-class reusable launch vehicles (alongside SpaceX's Falcon family). New Glenn is contracted for seven NSSL Phase 3 military launch missions worth $2.4 billion.
recent_developments.impact: Diversifies U.S. military launch capability beyond SpaceX-ULA duopoly, reducing single-provider risk and establishing competitive pressure that drives innovation and cost reduction.

recent_developments.headline: Space Force pauses Vulcan national security launches after booster anomaly
recent_developments.summary: Following ULA Vulcan's first national security launch on August 12, the Space Force issued an indefinite pause on Vulcan military missions after a repeated booster anomaly. ULA completed only one Vulcan launch in 2025 versus the planned ten. At least one national security mission was subsequently transferred to SpaceX, highlighting the fragility of the U.S. assured-access-to-space architecture.
recent_developments.impact: Vulcan's operational difficulties increase U.S. military dependence on SpaceX and underscore the importance of Blue Origin's New Glenn as a third launch provider for national security missions.

recent_developments.headline: U.S. Space Force awards $13.7 billion in NSSL Phase 3 contracts
recent_developments.summary: The Space Force awarded nearly $14 billion in National Security Space Launch Phase 3 Lane 2 contracts across three providers: SpaceX ($5.9 billion for 28 missions), ULA ($5.3 billion for 19 missions), and Blue Origin ($2.4 billion for 7 missions). This establishes the competitive framework for military launch services through the late 2020s, with SpaceX capturing the majority share.
recent_developments.impact: Establishes three-provider military launch architecture designed to ensure assured access to space, though Vulcan's subsequent operational issues have complicated this strategy.

risk_assessment.current_risk: The U.S. maintains a significant lead in space launch technology, primarily through SpaceX's unmatched launch cadence (165 orbital missions in 2025) and proven reusable launch capabilities. However, several risk factors are emerging. ULA Vulcan's operational difficulties have effectively reduced the assured-access-to-space architecture to a SpaceX-dependent model in the near term, until Blue Origin's New Glenn matures operationally. China's rapid launch cadence growth (from 68 to 92 launches in one year) and demonstrated multi-spaceport surge capacity indicate growing capability for responsive military space operations. While China's reusable rocket programs are 5-7 years behind SpaceX, the breadth of Chinese commercial and state investment (6+ companies pursuing reusability) suggests this gap could narrow. The Long March 9 super-heavy lift vehicle, expected by 2028-2029, will give China a capability class the U.S. currently leads with Starship.
key_drivers: SpaceX dominance provides unmatched U.S. launch capability but creates single-provider concentration risk
ULA Vulcan operational difficulties reduce assured access to space to near-single-provider dependency
China's 35% year-on-year launch growth (68 to 92 missions) and demonstrated surge capacity
Multiple Chinese commercial companies pursuing reusable rockets, indicating sustained investment momentum
Blue Origin's New Glenn success provides critical third U.S. launch provider but needs operational maturity
Starship development progress positions SpaceX for revolutionary capability but faces regulatory and technical hurdles
China's expanding military satellite constellations (Yaogan series) enabled by increasing launch capacity
mitigation_actions: Accelerate Blue Origin New Glenn operational certification for national security missions to reduce SpaceX dependency
Resolve ULA Vulcan booster anomaly to restore three-provider assured access to space architecture
Continue Starship development and certification for national security missions to achieve super-heavy lift capability
Invest in responsive space launch capabilities to match China's demonstrated multi-spaceport surge capacity
Develop on-orbit servicing and refueling capabilities to reduce dependence on launch cadence for constellation maintenance
Monitor Chinese reusable rocket progress and prepare for capability gap narrowing in 3-5 year timeframe
Expand Space Development Agency Proliferated Warfighter Space Architecture to outpace Chinese military constellation growth
